title: continue
description: Skipping just the current repetition with continue — the key difference from break, and the classic skip-even-numbers pattern.
objectives: Use continue to skip the rest of the current loop iteration
Understand the difference between continue and break
Know that continue still respects the loop's increment/condition
Recognize a common filtering pattern using continue

rows: Skips only the rest of the current repetition
The loop moves on to its next iteration as normal

sections.text: continue skips the remaining code in the current loop iteration and moves on to the loop's next check/increment, without exiting the loop.

sections.title: Tracing it
points: i = 1, 2 — condition i == 3 is false, cout runs
i = 3 — condition i == 3 is true, continue skips the cout line, jumps straight to i++
i = 4, 5 — loop resumes normally, cout runs again
3 never prints, but the loop still completes all 5 iterations — unlike break, which would have stopped it entirely

sections.text: 3. continue Still Respects the Loop's Own Mechanics

In a for loop, continue still triggers the increment before re-checking the condition — it doesn't skip that housekeeping, only the code written after it in the body. In a while or do-while loop, this means continue can accidentally cause an infinite loop if the increment happens after the continue in the body — worth double-checking.

Unlike a for loop, a while loop's increment is just an ordinary line in the body — if continue skips over it, i never changes, and the loop spins on i == 3 forever. When using continue in a while loop, make sure the variable that controls the condition is updated before the continue, or via some other path that isn't skipped.

This uses % (modulus) from Operators — i % 2 != 0 means "i is odd." continue skips odd numbers, leaving only even numbers printed. This is a very common shape for "process only items matching some condition, skip the rest."

sections.title: Grading a stack of papers
sections.text: Going through a stack of exam papers, you skip any paper marked "absent" — you don't stop grading altogether, you just move past that one paper to the next. That's continue: skip this one item, keep going through the rest.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using continue where break was actually meant — continue keeps looping, break stops it entirely; mixing them up gives very different results.
Placing continue in a while loop before the increment, causing an infinite loop when the condition variable never gets updated.
Assuming continue exits the loop early like break — it only skips ahead to the next iteration, the loop keeps running.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the difference between break and continue?
questions.answer: break exits the loop entirely, stopping all remaining iterations; continue skips only the rest of the current iteration and moves on to the next one.

questions.question: In a for loop, does continue skip the increment step?
questions.answer: No — the increment still runs before the condition is re-checked; continue only skips the code written after it in that iteration's body.

questions.question: Why can continue cause an infinite loop in a while loop specifically?
questions.answer: Because a while loop's increment is just an ordinary statement in the body — if continue is placed before it, that update gets skipped and the condition may never become false.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: continue skips the remaining code in the current loop iteration and moves on to the next one, without exiting the loop — unlike break, which stops the loop entirely. In a for loop, the increment still runs before continue's skip takes effect, so the loop mechanics stay intact. In a while loop, though, continue can cause an infinite loop if it's placed before the line that updates the condition's variable, since that update gets skipped. A common use is filtering: skipping items that don't match a condition while still processing the rest.

sections.title: Trick to Remember continue
sections.text: continue = skip this one, keep the loop going. break = stop the whole loop, right now. In while loops, make sure your increment isn't skipped by accident.
